skill.name: javascript-sdk
skill.description: JavaScript/TypeScript SDK for inference.sh - run AI apps, build agents, integrate 150+ models. Package: @inferencesh/sdk (npm install). Full TypeScript support, streaming, file uploads. Build agents with template or ad-hoc patterns, tool builder API, skills, human approval.
skill.summary: Build AI applications with the inference.sh JavaScript/TypeScript SDK
skill.author: inferen-sh

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation skill containing markdown files with code examples for the @inferencesh/sdk. Static scanner flagged 579 potential issues, but most are false positives from markdown code fences. The remaining findings are legitimate API usage patterns (network calls, environment variables) for an SDK that must communicate with inference.sh API. One notable pattern: eval() is used in code examples for a calculator tool demonstration without explicit input sanitization warnings.

high_findings.title: Code Examples Using eval() for Math Evaluation
high_findings.description: Tool builder documentation shows eval() used to evaluate math expressions from agent tool calls. While demonstrating a calculator tool pattern, eval() on user-controlled input is dangerous without sanitization. Users implementing this pattern should use safe evaluation libraries like math.js.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: eval() is explicitly present in code examples showing calculator tool implementation. The call.args.expression parameter comes from agent tool calls, making it user-influenced input.

medium_findings.title: Environment Variable Access for API Credentials
medium_findings.description: Code examples access process.env.INFERENCE_API_KEY for authentication. This is the recommended secure pattern for SDK usage, but documentation should clarify that users must provide their own API keys.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard SDK credential pattern. The skill is documentation, not executable code, so no actual credentials are exposed.

medium_findings.title: Webhook Tools with External API Endpoints
medium_findings.description: Documentation shows webhook tools calling external services (Slack, GitHub). These are legitimate integration patterns for the SDK.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard webhook pattern documented for extensibility. Secrets are stored server-side via .secret() method, not exposed client-side.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged 367 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' - these are markdown code fences (```bash, ```typescript) not actual shell commands. All files are .md documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All files are markdown documentation. Backtick patterns are code formatting, not executable code. Multiple files show this false positive pattern.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Flagged as Network Access
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged hardcoded URLs for inference.sh API and Slack webhooks. These are legitimate API endpoints documented for SDK integration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs are official inference.sh API endpoints documented for proper SDK usage. No malicious endpoints detected.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' patterns - these appear to be false positives from YAML frontmatter (---) and legitimate hash references.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No actual weak crypto implementations found. Frontmatter delimiters and documentation references triggered false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Code Evaluation with eval()
dangerous_patterns.description: eval() used in calculator tool examples without input sanitization demonstration
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Real eval() calls in code examples. While educational, users should be warned about the security implications and directed to safe alternatives like math.js.

content.user_title: Build AI Agents with the JavaScript SDK
content.value_statement: Integrate 150+ AI models into JavaScript/TypeScript applications with a unified SDK. Build agents, handle streaming responses, manage file uploads, and implement human approval workflows with full type safety.
actual_capabilities: Run AI apps with simple client.run() calls - supports 150+ models including Claude, GPT-4, and image generation
Build custom agents with tool definitions using the Tool Builder API (client tools, app tools, webhook tools, agent tools)
Handle real-time streaming responses with Server-Sent Events for progress updates
Manage file uploads and downloads with automatic handling and custom options
Implement stateful sessions for warm workers across multiple API calls
Integrate with frontend frameworks (Next.js, Express, React) using server-side proxy patterns
limitations: API key required - must be obtained from inference.sh and kept secure (not exposed in frontend code)
Browser usage requires server-side proxy to protect API credentials
Node.js 18+ required for full feature support (modern browsers work with basic fetch)
Rate limits and quotas depend on inference.sh API plan

use_cases.title: Build AI-Powered Node.js Services
use_cases.description: Create backend services that use AI for text generation, image creation, or complex task automation. Use sessions to maintain state and tools to integrate with your existing systems.

use_cases.title: Create AI Chat Interfaces in React
use_cases.description: Build chat UIs that leverage Claude or other models through the agent SDK. Handle streaming responses, display tool results, and implement human approval flows for sensitive operations.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers adding AI capabilities to web apps

use_cases.title: Automate Complex Workflows with Multi-Agent Systems
use_cases.description: Chain agents together with different capabilities. Use agent tools to delegate tasks, webhook tools to send notifications, and session management for long-running workflows.
use_cases.target_user: AI engineers building sophisticated automation pipelines

prompt_templates.title: Quick API Call
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the inference.sh SDK to call the flux-schnell model with the prompt: 'A sunset over mountains'. Log the result.

prompt_templates.title: Streaming Response Handler
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a streaming handler that processes real-time updates from the veo-3-1-fast model and logs each status change.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Tool with Approval
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a custom calculator tool using the tool builder API that requires human approval before executing expressions with eval().

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Agent Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a researcher agent and a coder agent. Have the researcher gather requirements and delegate code generation to the coder agent.

output_examples.input: Generate an image with 'A blue ocean at sunset'
output_examples.output: Task completed successfully. Image URL: https://cdn.inference.sh/... Output contains base64 image data and metadata including generation seed and processing time.

output_examples.input: Send message to agent asking about weather
output_examples.output: Agent response: 'The current weather in San Francisco is 72°F and sunny. Would you like me to look up the forecast for a different location?'

best_practices: Use environment variables for API keys: Set INFERENCE_API_KEY in your environment and access via process.env.INFERENCE_API_KEY. Never commit keys to version control.
Use server-side proxy for frontend apps: Route all inference.sh calls through your backend to keep API keys secure. The SDK provides Express, Next.js, and Hono middleware.
Enable human approval for sensitive operations: Use .requireApproval() on tools that perform destructive or expensive actions. Implement proper approval UI to let users confirm before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not embed API keys in frontend code: Frontend JavaScript is visible to users. Always proxy through your server or use secure secret management.
Do not skip error handling: API calls can fail for many reasons. Always wrap calls in try-catch and handle RequirementsNotMetException and InferenceError.
Do not use eval() without sanitization: The documentation shows eval() for calculator examples, but production code should use safe math libraries like math.js to prevent injection attacks.

faq.question: How do I get an API key for the inference.sh SDK?
faq.answer: Sign up at inference.sh, go to Settings, click API Keys, and create a new key. Keep it secure and never expose it in frontend code.

faq.question: Can I use this SDK in a browser without exposing my API key?
faq.answer: Yes. Configure your client with proxyUrl pointing to your backend endpoint. Your server-side code handles authentication with the API key.

faq.question: What models are available through this SDK?
faq.answer: The SDK supports 150+ models including Claude Sonnet 4, GPT-4o, image generation models like flux-schnell and veo-3-1-fast, and custom apps from the inference.sh registry.

faq.question: How does streaming work with the SDK?
faq.answer: Pass { stream: true } to client.run() and iterate with for await...of. Each update contains status, progress, and log messages from the running app.

faq.question: What is the difference between client tools and app tools?
faq.answer: Client tools execute code in your application. App tools call other inference.sh apps. Use client tools for custom logic, app tools to chain AI capabilities.

faq.question: How do I handle errors from the SDK?
faq.answer: Catch RequirementsNotMetException for missing input, InferenceError for API issues. Check e.errors array for specific validation problems in RequirementsNotMetException.
